item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it's owned by Barcroft TV (as indicated here) with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops didn't mean to reply

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one quite a bit, but two notes:

Idk why but I hate the "heh"

Also small typo "Not happy, obviously, what I mean was that I beginning to sort of go… numb" should have a was in there

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God damn this hit me hard for some reason.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: took a while for me to find this again, just wanted to comment on how deeply this resonates with me on a personal level. y'know, minus the controlled entropy and such. it's great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're the author, so your explanation for this is likely more valid than mine. However, I like mine better, so here you go:

When a reality bender creates something (+), it leaves behind the remnants of creation (-), following the traditional Newton's Laws. SCP-1915 alters reality so he stays forever the same, preventing anything but his expectations from happening. This kid wanted changed, even expected it at some point, but it never came; his own reality, including the self-wrought perceptions of his ego, was incapable of change.

I therefore choose to believe that this kid is an "Anti-" Reality Bender; he himself is the (-) to the (+) of 1915. Rather than happening on the (-) remnants, he found the missing parts of himself, and his (non-)existence finally became whole. And while this is still utterly sad, given that the kid believed himself to be a "true" person, I take the air of optimism from it; he finally got to go into space, to be "above and beyond" all that which he was never, ultimately, supposed to be a part of anyway. His "life," whatever qualities we assign to that, was finally complete.

+1. Either way, this is a gorgeous article, and I'm finding it to be one of the most beautiful expressions of emotion and evocation thereof that I've seen to-date on the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not what the caption says.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The hallucination is of the Earth from a specific place in orbit. The Foundation calculated where it was from and sent a satellite over to take a photo as an approximation to be included in the documentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The hell, I'm upvoting this!!! I like the sense of tragedy in it… Awesome +1, well deserved :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The entropy entity (heh, that's a fun phrase to say) and its interactions with the kid are evocative and fascinating. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How do you take a photograph of a hallucination?

Edit: I guess I will leave this as a no vote since I don't understand it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the view from the inside of the chamber.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand the photograph at all. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read this a while ago and thoroughly enjoyed it. No idea how I hadn't upvoted till now. Halfway to 100, let's hope that number keeps climbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Vastly underrated SCP. This should be the kind to break +100 easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I won't say that I liked this article, exactly - I came out of reading it in a worse mood than I did going in. However, the article was quite effective at doing this - so I upvoted. (Also, may I suggest specifying 'low earth orbit' rather than just 'in orbit')

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, I also found the note entirely unengaging. I struggled through it all, but I didn't care for the payoff either. The idea of an opposite to a reality bender is cool, but I don't think this implements it particularly well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the narration is long, it feels to me like he is just letting his feelings pour out while he still can. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was going to make the same point. How can there be a photograph of the view from the building if the view from the building is an (a?) hallucination? Did they send something up to the same point in orbit to take the photo?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congrats man, a great first draft turned into an even better article. I'm excited to see you posted it, and I disagree with people who find that the narrative is to long and subtracts from the quality of the article. Great job, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Her back has been" -> had

Also, this is pretty awesome. I sure liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's kind of amusing, as it's the same problem the protagonist had with his very existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Photo, sorry about the typo. So the effect makes them believe it's an hallucination?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A… proto? I'm not sure I follow. The main effect of the SCP is something the Foundation perceives as an hallucination. Beyond that, there is nothing hallucinatory about any of the narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, you did succeed in making me feel something so I'll give you the upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My issue is that you can't have a hallucination's proto because hallucinations happen in your mind. Unless I missed something, it means that was a rather bad mistake you've done here.

E: That last addendum is a serious wall of last will. And all I read was self-condescency. Not really atractive to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it seems this isn't going to work. I'll give it another 24 hours or so, and if the score on it doesn't change much (which is unlikely), I'll probably delete it. Sometimes experiments fail, I suppose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was my critique in chat. Dmatix politely but firmly refused to make the suggested changes. As such, I have to politely but firmly downvote. :/ (Not because he disagreed with me - because I think the length of the narrative destroys, rather than enhances, its effect.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the 1915 link is completely unnecessary, and frankly I tl;dr'd on the note or message or story or whatever that is. There's something that could be done with this concept, but I don't think this is it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for explaining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: downvote for negative worldview +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He absorbed the power that's released when a reality bender (here 1915) creates something. Since something is created from nothing, an opposite force (unmaking) also comes into being. Usually it's just released to harmful effects, but 1915 made sure it was safely stored for the narrator to find. When he absorved the power, however, he realized he couldn't use it for good, hence his ultimate conclusion to unmake himself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The narration really grabbed me, but I had some difficulty understanding what was going on after he met the hole with teeth. Did he become some sort of reality bender? Care to explain?

Still, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Er. I hate to comment on this, but you didn't actually upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You made an outstanding draft even better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw this on the forums.
I liked it then and I like it now.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing if not self-aware, eh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it yesterday. Still like it today. So, +1 : )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I boring you? Sorry if I am. I'm getting to some sort of point with this, I promise. It's just hard to keep focused, the way I am now.

Ha. I guess he does bore some people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perfectly understandable. It's not a particularly fun part to read. I do think it's important to get the point of the final decision across, but it can be off-putting for some.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry man. For what it is worth, I feel bad for even saying it… but I really can't get through so many paragraphs of being a loner at school.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To each his own. It is what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I keep trying to read the message that this whole thing was created around and I just literally cannot bring myself to care. I have tried three times and this huge mass of uninteresting words just keeps pushing me away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is likely my last Series 2 SCP, this is probably my most unusually written SCP article to date. The link with SCP-1915 was originally supposed to be implied, but after some deliberation I decided making it explicit would serve better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to see this got posted. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the editor kicked me out. I just did it myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was fixing the XXXXs per te, but you didn't let me do it :c
